email address : mmoulin@ifremer.fr Abstract : During the MAGIC (Margins of brAzil, Ghana, and Ivory Coast) experiment, five combined wide-angle, and reflection seismic profiles were acquired in the Pará-Maranhão/Barreirinhas/Ceará basins northern Brazil. This is a pull-apart passive margin, with two strike-slip borders. The equipment deployed includes 143 sea-bottom seismometers (OBS), a 4.5-km seismic streamer, and a 7587-in3 airgun array. In this paper, we focus on the distal parts of three profiles, and one entire transverse NW-SE profile, located on the presumed Cretaceous oceanic crust. Forward modelling of these wide-angle data sets reveals an E-W lateral evolution of the oceanic crust spreading initiation with: 1) just after the so-called intermediate domain, 60 km-wide domain that consists of a 5-km-thick crystalline crust. The basement presents two layers characterized by high acoustic velocity. This domain is bounded to the NW by a NW-SE volcanic line (Volcano Alignment), and 2) a 5- km-thick oceanic crust consisting of two layers characterized by “normal velocities” spanning between the two main fracture zones that fringe the Pará-Maranhão-Barreirinhas-Ceará segment. Despite a similar thickness, these two sub-domains present different velocity distribution in their two layers. They are both overlain by 5.5 km of sedimentary deposits. Forward wide-angle modelling confirms that the seafloor spreading process was progressive, with firstly the emplacement of a proto-oceanic crust, and then a thin oceanic crust. The “proto-oceanic” crust presents a similar seismic velocity with the intermediate domain interpreted as exhumed lower continental crust except for the lower part where the intruded lower crust 2 Please note that this is an author-produced PDF of an article accepted for publication following peer review. By contrast, the thin oceanic crust domain has a lower velocity structure in its upper layer, that is interpreted as basalt and is absent in the proto-oceanic crust. This eastward evolution, as also observed in the Provençal Basin, and the Santos Basin, suggests the involvement of the lower continental crust in the first steps of seafloor spreading. Highlights ► The Pará-Maranhão-Barreirinhas-Ceará passive margin in the Equatorial Atlantic segment presents a strong E-W-segmentation with: ► A transition to oceanic crust very complex, with the presence of an about 60 km-wide band of proto-oceanic crust before a “more typical” but still thin oceanic crust eastward. ► The passage between an intermediate domain and a typical oceanic crust is not abrupt in terms of composition but takes place in stages. ► This eastward evolution suggests the involvement of lower continental crust and/or continental mantle lithosphere erosion over time until the inception of « typical » oceanic crust. The MAGIC 57 experiment is a joint project of the Department of Marine Geosciences (IFREMER: 58 I F ç d R c c ’Ex tation de la MER, France), the Laboratory 59 of « Oceanic Geosciences » (IUEM: Institut Universitaire et Européen de la Mer, 60 France), the Faculdade de Ciências da Universidade de Lisboa (IDL, Portugal), the 61 Universidade de Brasilia (Brazil), and PETROBRAS (Brazil). The main goals of the 62 MAGIC experiment are (i) to investigate the deep structure of the -Maranhão- 63 Barreirinhas-Ceará basins, N-NE Brazil, (ii) to characterize the segmentation and the 64 nature of the crust in the different domains of this passive margin, between the 65 Jo Moulin et al., revised version 3 / 27 unthinned continental crust and the true oceanic crust, and (iii) to understand the 66 fundamental processes that lead to the thinning, and finally to the breakup of the 67 continental crust in the specific context of a pull-apart system with two strike-slip 68 borders. The 76 Equatorial Atlantic Ocean can itself be interpreted as a "transfer zone" between two 77 main oceans resulting from two ruptures during two global geodynamic revolutions 78 (Moulin & Aslanian, 2010; Leroux et al., 2018): the opening of the Central Atlantic 79 Ocean that started by Sinemurian time (Sahabi et al., 2004), and the opening of the 80 South Atlantic Ocean in Hauterivian time (Rabinowitz & LaBrecque 1979; Austin & 81 Uchupi 1982; Curie 1984; Moulin et al. 2010a), which started about 60 My later and 82 shifted about 30° to the east respect to the Central Atlantic. 83 Despite the lack of magnetic lineaments due to its position close to the equator, the 84 beginning of seafloor spreading on this portion of the ocean is dated to Cretaceous 85 time, separating Africa from South America. The seismic source consisted of a tuned array of 140 18 airguns ranging from 250 in3 G-guns to 9 L Bolt airguns, with main frequencies 141 centred around 10-15 Hz. Shots occurred at a constant time interval of 60 s (firing 142 rate), resulting in intervals of about 150 m between shots. A total of 12382 shots 143 (profile MC-1: 3032, MC-2: 1741, MC-3: 2834, MC-4: 1145, and MC-5: 3801) were 144 fired by the air gun array, recorded simultaneously on MCS reflection and wide-angle 145 seismic profiles. 146 This paper focuses on profile MC1 and the distal parts of crossing profiles MC2, MC3 147 and MC4. The MC1 profile (Figure 1) is about 360 km long and is located entirely on 148 the presumed oceanic-floored basin, except on its south-easternmost part. It spans the 149 entire domain between the two main fracture zones that fringe the -Maranhão- 150 Barreirinhas-Ceará segment: the São Paulo Double Fracture Zone (FZ) to the north 151 and the Romanche FZ to the south. The processing sequence of the reflection seismic data was 176 composed of geometry (including streamer feathering), CMP binning at 12.5 m 177 intervals and sorting, bandpass filter (2–16–64–96 Hz), re-sampling from 2 to 4 ms. 178 After velocity analysis, true amplitude recovery was applied plus normal move-out, 179 multiple attenuation, time-variant bandpass filter (from 2–16–48–64 at sea bottom to 180 2–16–32–48 Hz 3 s below), inside and outside mute, stack and post-stack time-variant 181 bandpass filter and Kirchhoff time migration. 182 183 4. Forward Modelling 184 The data were modelled by using an iterative procedure of two-dimensional forward 185 ray-tracing followed by a damped least-squares travel-time inversion with the 186 RAYINVR software (Zelt & Smith, 1992). The wide-angle modelling proceeds in a 187 top-to-down strategy of arrival time fitting of the reflected and refracted phases 188 identified in the sedimentary section. were modelled by using an iterative procedure of two-dimensional forward 185 ray-tracing followed by a damped least-squares travel-time inversion with the 186 RAYINVR software (Zelt & Smith, 1992). The wide-angle modelling proceeds in a 187 top-to-down strategy of arrival time fitting of the reflected and refracted phases 188 identified in the sedimentary section. For each sedimentary sequence, we correlated 189 the two-way travel times of its base, from the MCS section, with the arrival times of 190 the reflected and refracted phases identified in the OBS data. 191 An iterative procedure of velocity and depth adjustment, with control of the depth-twtt 192 (two way travel-time) conversion against MCS data, was then applied. This procedure 193 was applied to all sedimentary layers down to the basement. For the basement, we 194 only used arrival times from the OBS and LSS data set. The iterative procedure stops 195 when an appropriate fit of the arrival times from both OBS and MCS data is reached, 196 i.e., the stopping criteria is a normalized chi-square of 1. 197 To constrain velocity gradients and velocity contrasts at the interfaces of the model, 198 Journal Pre-proof Moulin et al., revised version 7 / 27 we performed amplitude modelling (Zelt & Ellis, 1988), which is a trial and error 199 procedure. Amplitude modelling is based on the variation of the angle of incidence of 200 the transmitted and reflected seismic energy across boundaries; therefore, synthetic 201 record sections should reproduce the observed offset variations of amplitude. 202 Furthermore, for each incidence angle, the fractions of transmitted and reflected P- 203 wave energy (relative to the total incident energy) depend on the impedance contrast 204 across interfaces; thus, synthetic record sections should reproduce the relative 205 amplitudes amongst all the arrivals. Another important constraint provided by the 206 amplitude modelling is the thickness and velocity gradient in each layer. 207 The final velocity model of the MC1 profile images the sedimentary and crustal layers 208 to a depth of around 20 km (Figure 8). The model is composed of five sedimentary 209 layers (S1 to S5), 5.5 km thick in total, with top and bottom seismic velocities of 1.8 210 and 2.2 km/s (S1), 2.4 and 2.75 km/s (S2), 2.9 and 3.0 km/s (S3), 3.75 km/s (S4), and 211 4.15 and 4.2 km/s (S5). Near the intersection with the Romanche FZ (at a distance of 212 280 km form the model), the velocity increases in layer S4 (4.5 to 4.75 km/s) and 213 layer S5 (5.0 to 5.5 km/s). The velocity increases gradually inside the sedimentary 214 column and no velocity inversion is identified in this profile, in contrast to what is 215 observed, westwards, in the deep basin of the Pará-Maranhão/Barreirinhas/Ceará 216 margin (Aslanian et al,. 2021). Comparison to reflection seismic data 274 The MAGIC1 MCS profile starts in the middle of the continental slope with ~3 s twtt 275 sedimentary layers (Figure 11). These sediment layers thicken to 3.5 s twtt into the 276 distal part (Northwards). The four shallowest layers are well individualised and 277 stratified, but the absence of well-imaged reflectors in the fifth one makes it difficult 278 to define its top. Nevertheless, the top of the acoustic basement and reflections from 279 the Moho are well-marked by a strong reflector at 8 s, and a discontinuous one at 9 s 280 twtt, between 160 and 280 km model distance.
